Then one morning, as the hunter-chief of the village walked along the frozen shore, he saw a dark shape against the white snow. It was a duck—a black duck—sitting on a rock at the edge of the ice. The hunter crept closer, his bow ready, but to his astonishment, the duck spoke to him. "Do not shoot me, hunter," said the black duck. "I am not here to be your meal. I am here to teach you how to survive."

The hunter lowered his bow and listened. The black duck told him that the winter would be long but that the people could endure it if they learned to adapt as the duck did. "Look at my feathers," said the black duck. "They are black like the rock of the shore and the bark of the cedar. When I sit still against the stone, the predators cannot see me. You must learn to move quietly through the forest as I do, to use your surroundings as cover." The duck then showed the hunter how to find the small pockets of open water where fish still swam, how to read the ice for signs of weakness, and how to follow the tracks of animals that knew the secret paths through the deep snow.

In some versions of this tale, the black duck is one of many animals that the great trickster Wisakedjak or Nanabozho tries to deceive. In these stories, Wisakedjak builds a lodge and invites the ducks to a dance, telling them to close their eyes. He then wrings their necks one by one. But the black duck, wiser than the others, suspects the trick and escapes, teaching the people never to follow blindly. The black duck's dark feathers, which allow it to disappear against the winter landscape, became its gift—a reminder that camouflage and patience are as valuable as strength.

In another telling, the black duck is Makadeshib, a brave Ojibwe war chief who led his people against their enemies in the early nineteenth century near what is now North Dakota. When his war party was surrounded and outnumbered, Makadeshib and a small group of warriors stayed behind to fight so the others could escape. They removed their clothes, smoked their pipes, prayed to the manitou, and faced their enemies with calm courage. Each of the thirteen who stayed died fighting, but their sacrifice saved the rest. A white cross was later erected where they fell.

The black duck teaches that true strength is not in raw power but in knowing when to fight, when to hide, and when to adapt. The people who heard this story learned to respect the duck, to observe its ways, and to carry its lessons with them through every winter. And whenever a black duck is seen sitting motionless against a dark rock by the shore, it is said that the spirit of the first black duck is still there, watching, remembering, and waiting to share its wisdom with those willing to listen.
moral: Don't kill.

source_references.title: Canadian Folk Tales from the Maritime Provinces
source_references.author: Hermann M. Belden

title: The Tale of the First Fire
summary: In the beginning, when the world was young and the animals spoke with human voices, there was no fire on earth.
full_text:
In the beginning, when the world was young and the animals spoke with human voices, there was no fire on earth. The people and the animals shivered through long, cold nights. They ate their food raw, for there was no way to cook. The winters were harsh, and without fire's warmth, many suffered. But fire existed—the Thunders, the great Sky People who lived above the arch of the heavens, had fire. They kept it in a hollow sycamore tree on an island, guarded by the waters. The animals could see the smoke rising from the distant tree, but they did not know how to cross the water and bring the fire back.

The Cherokee people tell this story most vividly. The animals held a great council to decide who would venture across the water. The Raven, proud of his strength and size, stepped forward first. "I will go," he said. "I am large and strong. The fire will not defeat me." He flew high across the water and alighted on the sycamore tree. But as he sat there, wondering how to carry the fire back, the heat scorched his feathers black. Frightened and singed, he returned without the fire, and from that day forward, all ravens have had black feathers.

Next, the council sent the Screech Owl. He flew to the island and peered down into the hollow tree. A blast of hot air shot up, nearly burning out his eyes. He flew home in pain, and ever since, Screech Owls have had red eyes from the heat.

The Hooting Owl and the Horned Owl volunteered together. They reached the tree, but the smoke was so thick it nearly blinded them, and the wind carried ashes that formed white rings around their eyes. They returned without the fire, and neither has ever been able to rub away those white rings.

Now the birds were too afraid to try. So the snakes volunteered. Little Snake, the black racer, swam across the water and crawled through the grass to the tree. He entered through a small hole at the bottom, but the smoke and heat were too much. He escaped, but his body was scorched black, and he was so twisted from his desperate escape that he has moved in zigzags ever since. The great black snake, the Climber, tried next. He climbed the outside of the tree and looked down into the hole, but the smoke choked him, and he fell into the burning stump. When he finally crawled out, he too was black, and he has been the black snake ever since.

One by one, all the animals had failed. The council despaired. The world remained cold and dark. Then a small voice spoke up. It was the Water Spider—not the mosquito-like water strider, but the Water Spider with black, downy hair and red stripes on her body, who can run on top of water or dive beneath it. "I will go," she said. The other animals laughed. "You are so small," they said. "How will you carry fire?" The Water Spider said, "I will manage." She spun a thread from her body and wove it into a small bowl—a tusti bowl—which she fastened to her back. She skittered across the surface of the water, her eight legs carrying her swiftly to the island. She crawled through the grass to the sycamore tree, and there she took one small coal of fire and placed it in the bowl on her back. She crossed back over the water, and the fire was saved.

From that day forward, the people have had fire. And the Water Spider still carries her tusti bowl on her back. You can see it to this day if you look closely.

Other First Nations tell similar stories. The Algonquin tell of Wisakedjak who tricked the ducks and geese into dancing with their eyes closed so he could kill them for their fat, which he used to steal fire. The Salish tell of how Beaver stole fire from the Sky People and hid it in his tail. On the West Coast, Raven is the fire-bringer, stealing it from the Owner-of-the-Fire and hiding it in a cedar tree.

But no matter how the story is told, the lesson is the same: fire is a gift, hard-won and sacred. It came to humanity through the courage and cleverness of the smallest and most humble of creatures—not the strongest or the fastest, but the one who found a way. The Water Spider teaches us that size and strength are not the only measures of ability. Often, it is the smallest among us who accomplish the greatest things.
moral: Fire is shared.

source_references.title: Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 15: Northeast
source_references.author: Bruce Trigger (ed.)
source_references.note: Covers Algonquian and Iroquoian fire-bringing narratives and ceremonial traditions.

source_references.title: Algonquian Spirit: Contemporary Translations of the Algonquian Literatures
source_references.author: Brian Swann (ed.)
source_references.note: Translations of oral narratives including fire origin stories from Anishinaabe and other Algonquian peoples.

title: The Tale of the Bak Bak
summary: In the islands of the Philippines, where the coconut palms sway against turquoise skies and the sea stretches endlessly in every direction, the elders tell stories of the Bak Bak — a mysterious creature that lives in the deepest parts of the forest.
full_text:
In the islands of the Philippines, where the coconut palms sway against turquoise skies and the sea stretches endlessly in every direction, the elders tell stories of the Bak Bak — a mysterious creature that lives in the deepest parts of the forest. Its name comes from the sound it makes: a hollow, rhythmic knocking, like wood striking wood, that echoes through the trees at twilight.

The Bak Bak was not always a creature of the forest. According to the tale, she was once a beautiful maiden named Luisa who lived in a coastal village. She was proud and vain, mocking the poorer girls and refusing to share her family's abundance. When a beggar woman came to her door asking for food, Luisa laughed and slammed the door in her face.

That beggar woman was a powerful sorceress in disguise. She cursed Luisa: "You who refused to share will become a hollow thing, knocking forever on doors that will never open." Luisa's body withered and transformed. Her arms became long and thin, her fingers hardened into knuckles like wood, and her voice turned into a hollow knock. She fled into the forest, ashamed.

Now the Bak Bak wanders the forest at dusk, knocking on trees and rocks, making a sound like someone asking to be let in. Children are warned not to go into the forest after dark, lest they hear the Bak Bak's knock and be led astray. But there is another side to the story: if you hear the Bak Bak and offer food, the curse will lift, and she will bless your household with abundance. The tale teaches that generosity is always rewarded and that selfishness hollows the soul from within.
moral: The dead who are wronged will rise.

title: Teke Teke: The Railway Ghost
summary: A woman who died on the railway returns as a vengeful spirit, her body sliced in half, hunting the living.
full_text:
The railway lines of Japan are veins of steel and electricity that pulse with the frantic energy of millions. But in the dead of night, when the last train has departed and the stations are empty, these tracks become the hunting grounds of a nightmare. They call her Teke Teke, a name derived not from a word, but from the sound of her movement—the rhythmic, scraping noise of elbows hitting concrete.

Teke Teke was once a schoolgirl, full of dreams and laughter, until a singular, horrific accident tore her world apart. The stories vary—some say she fell by mistake, others say she was pushed—but the result was the same. A speeding train sliced through her body, severing her at the waist. She did not die instantly; she lay on the cold gravel of the tracks, screaming for help, while the station staff, paralyzed by shock or indifference, failed to save her in time. In her final moments, her terror morphed into a concentrated, obsidian hatred. She died not as a girl, but as an Onryō—a vengeful spirit bound to the place of her mutilation.

Now, she roams the urban periphery, a creature of halves. She has no legs, only a torso that she drags behind her with a strength that defies physics. As she moves, her elbows strike the ground: *teke, teke, teke*. To the unsuspecting ear, it sounds like a distant, rhythmic tapping, almost benign. But if you hear that sound when you are alone at night, you have already entered her orbit.

Teke Teke is a relentless predator. She does not tire, and she does not stop. If she spots a victim, she accelerates with terrifying speed, her upper body gliding across the pavement like a blade. Those who try to outrun her find that she is always faster, the *teke-teke* sound growing louder and more frantic until she is right behind them. Her primary weapon is a great, rusted scythe, or sometimes her own jagged nails, which she uses to slice her victims in half at the waist. She does not kill for hunger, but for symmetry; she ensures that her victims share her own grotesque disfigurement.

There is a darker layer to the legend: the story of Kashima Reiko. Some say Teke Teke is a manifestation of Kashima, a spirit who haunts the bathrooms of schools, asking those she encounters, "Where are my legs?" If the answer is wrong, the result is the same: a sudden, violent bisection.

The legend of Teke Teke serves as a grim reminder of the fragility of life and the enduring power of resentment. It warns against the indifference of strangers and the permanence of loss. In the quiet hours of the night, when the wind howls through the railway gantries, the people of Japan remember to walk quickly and never look back. For in the shadows of the station, something is scraping the ground, searching for someone to join her in her half-lived existence.
moral: Violent death leaves souls unable to rest in peace.

title: Tokoloshe: The Water Imp
summary: A mischievous, malevolent water spirit that can only be seen by certain people—and is used to frighten children.
full_text:
In the vibrant, rhythmic landscapes of South Africa, among the Zulu people, there is a creature that exists in the periphery of vision—a small, malevolent spirit known as the Tokoloshe. The Tokoloshe is a dwarf-sized imp, with a bloated head, a wrinkled hide, and a penchant for chaos that borders on the obsessive.

The Tokoloshe is a creature of the water, emerging from rivers and dams to enter the human world. For most, the Tokoloshe is invisible, a whisper in the dark or a sudden chill in a warm room. But some are born with the "sight," able to see the imp as it scuttles across the floor or perches on the edge of a bed.

The Tokoloshe is not a predator of flesh, but a predator of peace. It is a master of mischief: it hides keys, spills milk, and whispers lies into the ears of sleeping people to sow discord in the family. But its most dreaded habit is its nocturnal visits. The Tokoloshe is said to ride on the chests of sleepers, inducing nightmares and a suffocating pressure that leaves the victim exhausted and drained upon waking.

Because of this, a unique architectural tradition emerged in some Zulu households. To protect themselves from the Tokoloshe, families began to raise their beds on bricks, creating a gap between the mattress and the floor. The belief is that the Tokoloshe, being small and lacking the agility to jump high, cannot reach the sleeper if the bed is sufficiently elevated. A charm of herbs or a small mirror placed above the door also serves to deter the imp, as the Tokoloshe is said to be fascinated by its own reflection.

In some communities, the Tokoloshe is viewed as a tool of witchcraft. It is believed that an envious neighbor can "hire" a Tokoloshe to plague another family, using the spirit to bring sickness or financial ruin. This makes the Tokoloshe a symbol of the social tensions and hidden animosities that exist within any community.

The legend of the Tokoloshe is a blend of genuine fear and cultural storytelling. It provides a tangible explanation for the "night terrors" and sleep paralysis that affect many people. But more than that, it serves as a reminder of the unseen forces that shape our lives. The Tokoloshe teaches us that the world is larger and stranger than we perceive, and that sometimes, the best way to deal with a demon is simply to raise your bed a few inches higher.
moral: Even fictional warnings teach caution.

title: Thoth
summary: In the grand hall of judgment that lies at the heart of the Duat, the Egyptian underworld, there sits a figure of profound importance.
full_text:
In the grand hall of judgment that lies at the heart of the Duat, the Egyptian underworld, there sits a figure of profound importance. He has the body of a man and the head of an ibis, his long curved beak poised over a reed brush, his eyes fixed on the scales of justice that balance before him. He is Thoth, the scribe of the gods, the lord of writing, the master of magic, the keeper of time, and the wisest of all the Egyptian deities.

Thoth's origins are as mysterious as the knowledge he guards. Some texts say he was born from the lips of Ra, the sun god, at the dawn of creation. Others claim he created himself through the power of his own word. In Egyptian mythology, Thoth is both a god and a cosmic principle—he is the mind that organizes the universe, the intelligence that brings order out of chaos. Without Thoth, creation would be formless and meaningless, a chaos of unspoken possibilities.

His primary role is that of divine scribe. Thoth records everything that happens in the universe. Every thought, every word, every deed is written down in his celestial scrolls. He is the chronicler of existence, the librarian of the cosmos, the one who ensures that nothing is ever truly lost. When the pharaohs of Egypt performed their great deeds, they did so with the hope that Thoth would record them for eternity. When a scribe sat down to copy a sacred text, he prayed to Thoth to guide his hand.

But Thoth's most important duty takes place in the Hall of Maat, the hall of judgment, where the souls of the dead are weighed against the feather of truth. The deceased's heart is placed on one side of a scale, the white feather of Maat representing truth and justice on the other. Anubis, the jackal-headed god of embalming, adjusts the weights. Osiris presides as judge. But it is Thoth who records the verdict. If the heart balances perfectly with the feather, the soul is worthy and may enter the Field of Reeds, a paradise of eternal peace. But if the heart is heavier than the feather, weighted down by sins and wrongdoing, the soul is devoured by Ammit, the devourer—a terrible creature with the head of a crocodile, the body of a lion, and the hindquarters of a hippopotamus.

Thoth's judgment is final. He is utterly impartial, recording the truth without favor or prejudice. The spells and formulas known as the Egyptian Book of the Dead were designed, in part, to help the deceased pass this judgment. Many of these spells were attributed to Thoth himself, who was believed to have written the sacred texts that guided souls through the perils of the underworld.

Beyond his role in judgment, Thoth is also the god of magic, or heka. In Egyptian belief, magic was not trickery or illusion but the fundamental creative force of the universe. The gods had created the world through words of power, and those who understood those words could reshape reality. Thoth was the master of these words, the keeper of the spells that could command the forces of nature, heal the sick, and even raise the dead. The most famous magical text of ancient Egypt, the Book of Thoth, was said to contain spells that would allow the reader to understand the language of animals, to see the gods, and to grasp the very structure of the universe. But the book was also said to be guarded by deadly serpents, and those who sought it risked their lives.

Thoth is also associated with the moon. He balances Ra, the sun god, providing light in the darkness, measuring the passage of time, and counting the days, months, and years. The lunar calendar, with its cycles of waxing and waning, was Thoth's invention. He is often depicted with a crescent moon on his head, a symbol of his mastery over time and his role as the measurer of all things.

In the Egyptian pantheon, Thoth is the god who bridges the gap between the human and the divine. He invented hieroglyphics, the sacred writing that allowed humans to communicate with the gods and to preserve their thoughts for eternity. He taught humanity mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and law. He is the patron of all scribes, scholars, and scientists, the original intellectual who valued knowledge above all else.

The Greeks, encountering Egyptian culture through the filter of their own mythology, identified Thoth with their god Hermes, the messenger and trickster. From this fusion came Hermes Trismegistus, "thrice-great Hermes," a legendary figure who was said to have written a vast body of esoteric texts on alchemy, astrology, and magic. These Hermetic texts became foundational to Western occultism, and Thoth's influence thus spread far beyond the banks of the Nile.

In the end, Thoth represents something that transcends Egyptian mythology. He is the embodiment of the idea that knowledge is sacred, that writing is divine, that the act of recording and remembering is itself a form of immortality. In a world where the spoken word vanishes like wind, Thoth gives us the written word, which lasts forever. He is the god who ensures that when we die, our deeds are remembered, our names are recorded, and our stories live on.
moral: Words have power—use them wisely.

source_references.title: The Search for God in Ancient Egypt
source_references.author: Jan Assmann

title: Tao and the Mountain
summary: In ancient times, during the Warring States period, two great mountains stood between the southern part of Jizhou and the northern bank of the Yellow River.
full_text: In ancient times, during the Warring States period, two great mountains stood between the southern part of Jizhou and the northern bank of the Yellow River. The first was called Taihang, and the second was called Wangwu. They were not ordinary hills; they were vast, towering masses of rock and forest that rose like walls between the provinces, forcing travelers to walk hundreds of li around them to reach the other side. At the northern foot of these mountains lived an old man named Yu Gong, which meant Foolish Old Man. He was nearly ninety years old, his beard white as snow and his back bent like a bow, but his eyes still held the stubborn light of a man who had never accepted defeat. The mountains blocked his family's path to the south. Every time they needed to visit the market or see relatives, they had to make a journey of many days around the base of the peaks. "This is intolerable," Yu Gong announced one morning, as he watched his sons pack provisions for yet another long detour. "I am going to remove these mountains." His wife laughed. "You cannot even move a pebble," she said. "What makes you think you can level two mountains? And where would you put the earth and the rocks?" "We will carry them to the edge of the Bohai Sea," Yu Gong replied. "The sea is vast. It will not refuse a few basketfuls of dirt." His sons, though skeptical, loved their father and agreed to help. Even a young widow's child of seven or eight, who lived nearby, came along to lend his small hands. The next morning, they began. Armed with picks, shovels, and bamboo baskets, Yu Gong and his family climbed the slopes of Taihang Mountain and began to chip away at its ancient stone. They filled their baskets with earth and rock, carried them down to the shore of the Bohai Sea, dumped them into the water, and then walked all the way back to the mountain. The journey took them one full year, there and back. A wise old man named Zhi Sou, whose name meant Clever Old Man, came to watch them. He stood on a ridge with his hands on his hips and laughed until his sides ached. "You are the biggest fool I have ever seen," he called down to Yu Gong. "You are ninety years old, weak as a winter fly, and you cannot even bend a blade of grass. How do you expect to move these great mountains?" Yu Gong straightened his back with difficulty and looked up at the mocking man. "You are more stubborn than a rock," he said, "and less wise than the child who helps me. Listen to me, Clever Old Man. When I die, I have sons. When my sons die, they have grandsons. My grandsons will have sons of their own. Generation after generation, there is no end to my descendants. But these mountains will not grow any taller. Every basket I remove makes them smaller. So tell me, why can I not move them?" Zhi Sou had no answer. He shook his head and walked away, muttering about fools and wasted effort. Yu Gong and his family continued their work. They chipped and carried, day after day, year after year. The mountain god, who lived deep in the heart of Taihang, grew alarmed. This foolish old man was actually making progress. At this rate, in a few thousand years, the entire mountain might be gone. The god reported the matter to the Emperor of Heaven. The Emperor looked down from his celestial palace and saw the old man, tiny as an ant, chipping away at the vast stone face of the mountain. He saw the patient rhythm of the picks, the steady march of the basket carriers, the unyielding determination in Yu Gong's ancient eyes. The Emperor was moved. "Such sincerity cannot be ignored," he said. He summoned the two sons of the god Kua'e, giants of immense strength, and commanded them to descend to earth. That night, while Yu Gong and his family slept, the two giants lifted Taihang Mountain and Wangwu Mountain onto their shoulders and carried them away. One mountain was placed in the far east of Shu, and the other was set down in the far north of Yong. From that day forward, the road from Jizhou to the southern bank of the Hanshui River was clear and level. Yu Gong woke the next morning to find an open plain where the mountains had stood. He smiled, said a quiet prayer of thanks, and went back to his work, for there was always something that needed doing.
moral: Self-sacrifice for others is the highest virtue.

source_references.title: Vietnamese Supernaturalism: Views from the Chinese Coast
source_references.author: Pelley, Patricia M.

title: Trolls in Church
summary: In the mountains of Norway, where the trolls have their halls in the great stone halls, there live creatures who see humans as pests—annoying, but sometimes useful.
full_text:
In the mountains of Norway, where the trolls have their halls in the great stone halls, there live creatures who see humans as pests—annoying, but sometimes useful. What the trolls cannot understand is why humans need churches, those small stone buildings where they gather to sing and speak of gods. Some trolls are curious; some are envious. And some, the tales say, wanted what the humans had.

It was well-known in the valley villages that trolls could not enter a church. The holy sign above the door, the consecrated ground within—these were barriers that no troll could cross. But curiosity drives even the mountain folk, and one midwinter's eve, a troll-woman decided to see for herself.

She wrapped herself in a great white sheet—thinking to look like a human woman come to worship. The sexton, an old man named Lars, saw her come in. Even in the candlelight, he could see she was huge—her shadow filled the whole doorway. Her face, though passing for human, had something wrong about it.

She gave him a bundle—a child, she said, to be christened. But Lars saw, in the flickering light, that the child was not right. It was heavy, and its skin was cold. He baptised it anyway, using Gods name. The child screamed—a terrible sound, more animal than human—then went still. It was a changeling, a troll child left to be raised as human. But the baptism had killed it. The troll-woman fled into the night, and Lars was never troubled again.

Another tale tells of trolls who grew bold enough to attack. They came down from the mountains one Sunday morning, their stone feet shaking the earth, determined to tear down the little church that dared to stand on their mountain. But the priest was quick. He rang the great iron bell—the sanctus bell, rung during mass. The sound, blessed and holy, struck the trolls like a physical blow. Where they stood, they became stone. To this day, there are strange stone formations on the mountain slopes that the folk call the Trolls' Church.

This is why churches are safe against trolls—the divine protects its own. And why bells matter: ringing them drives back the darkness. The trolls of Norway, for all their strength, cannot stand against the power of holiness.
moral: The divine protects.

source_references.title: Svenska Folksagor
source_references.author: Herman Hofberg
source_references.notes: Widely attested Swedish legend type (TNT 3052): trolls caught in church at dawn

title: The Tomte and the Christmas Porridge
summary: A farmer learns to respect the farm spirit and the importance of keeping traditions.
full_text:
In the province of Dalarna, where the winters are long and the traditions run deep, there lived a farmer named Erik who considered himself a man of reason. He had no time for old wives' tales or the superstitions of his ancestors. He worked hard, he said, and his success came from his own efforts—not from any spirits or unseen forces.

Every Christmas, Eriks neighbors performed the old rituals. They left a bowl of porridge on the hearth, first for the Tomte—the farm spirit, the little red-capped guardian of the barn and the animals. They spoke words of respect, of thanks for the year's protection. Erik laughed at them. Feeding the air!' he would say. If there were Tomte, I'd have seen them.

Christmas Eve came, and with it, a bitter cold. Erik was warm in his house, listening to his wife worry about the animals. He shrugged off her concerns—let the Tomte worry, if they existed.

At midnight, he heard a terrible commotion in the barn. Running out into the snow, he saw destruction: the barn doors torn from their hinges, his cows standing bewildered in the cold, and in the moonlight, a small figure. It wore a red cap and had a long white beard. Its eyes blazed with anger.

'No porridge for me? the Tomte cried. 'No offering, no thanks? Then no protection for you!

Erik tried to apologize, but the spirit vanished. The next year was disastrous. The cows fell ill. The milk turned sour. The horses went lame. One of Erik's sons broke his leg. Nothing went right, no matter how hard he worked.

At the next Christmas, Erik was a changed man. He made the finest porridge, the creamiest, the sweetest. He placed it on the hearth with proper words of gratitude. In the morning, the porridge was gone—and a small wooden toy sat in its place, a gift for his youngest child. His farm prospered from that day forward.

The lesson: respect traditions, for they protect what we love. The unseen have power to help or hinder.
moral: Respect traditions—they protect what we love.

title: Tiddalik the Frog
summary: The greedy frog who drank all the water, and had to be made to laugh to release it.
full_text:
In the Dreamtime, when the world was new, there lived a frog—Tiddalik. He was fat, round, and green, with eyes that bulged from his head. But more than anything, he was greedy.

All the animals were thirsty. The rivers were low, the waterholes were drying. But there was water—there was enough, if everyone shared.

Tiddalik did not want to share. He crawled to the biggest waterhole, the one that fed every stream. He opened his great mouth and drank. He drank until his belly was huge, round as a hill. He drank until there was no water left.

The animals gathered, crying for water. The emu cried. The kangaroo cried. The lizards and snakes cried. All the creatures of the bush cried out—but there was no water.

A wise old crow had an idea. 'Make him laugh, the crow said. 'If he laughs, he will release the water.

The animals tried—making faces, telling jokes, dancing. Nothing worked. Tiddalik just sat on his swollen belly, greedy and still.

But there was one thing they had not tried. A clever little man—a dwarf, some called him—began to dance. He danced the funniest dance the world had ever seen. He tripped, he fell, he made sounds that had no names.

Tiddalik laughed—his great belly shook. The water came rushing out—rivers, streams, waterholes, all full again. The animals drank, they played, they lived.

Tiddalik learned nothing. He remains greedy to this day—waiting for someone to make him laugh, waiting for his next drink. But the water keeps coming back.

The lesson: greed does not last—but laughter does. Do not be greedy, or you will be the one left dry when the water returns.
moral: Greed leaves you empty—laughter fills the world.
source_references: Robinson, George. Australian Aboriginal Folk Tales. Kangaroo Press, 1980; Berndt, Ronald M. and Catherine H. The Speaking Land. Penguin, 1989

title: Thanh Gióng the Giant Saint
summary: In the folklore of the Philippines, there is a creature that every child learns about before they can walk.
full_text:
In the folklore of the Philippines, there is a creature that every child learns about before they can walk. She is the Manananggal, a vampire-like monster that can separate her upper body from her lower body. By night, she flies through the air, her wings like those of a giant bat, her entrails trailing behind her like gruesome streamers.

The Manananggal appears during the day as a normal woman. She might be a neighbor, a friend, a stranger. But at night, she rubs a special oil on her body, and her torso splits at the waist. Her upper body grows bat-like wings, and she takes to the sky, leaving her lower half on the ground.

She hunts for pregnant women. The Manananggal is attracted to the scent of childbirth. She lands on rooftops and extends her long, hollow tongue — like the proboscis of a mosquito — through cracks in the thatch. She pierces the belly of the sleeping pregnant woman and sucks the blood of the unborn child.

The only way to stop a Manananggal is to find her lower half and destroy it. Place salt or garlic on the exposed stump, and when the sun rises, the upper half will not be able to rejoin her body. She will die at dawn, caught between her two halves.

The Manananggal is more than a simple vampire story. She represents a deep fear in Filipino culture: the fear of the trusted person who is secretly a monster. In a society built on close family and community ties, the idea that a neighbor or relative could be a supernatural predator is terrifying.

There are also practical protections against the Manananggal. Pregnant women are advised to sleep with a knife under their pillow. A crucifix or a string of garlic cloves above the door is said to keep her away. And if you see a bright light moving through the trees at night — a ball of flame hovering in the darkness — cover your mouth. The Manananggal is searching, and she can hear a gasp from a mile away.
moral: ordinary children become extraordinary heroes for just causes.

source_references.title: The Saint of the South: Legend and History in Vietnamese Folk Culture
source_references.author: Nguyen The Anh

title: To-Kabinana and To-Karvuvu: The Twin Gods of New Britain
summary: Two divine brothers create the world through wisdom and folly, explaining the origins of good and evil.
full_text:
On the island of New Britain, part of the archipelago of Papua New Guinea, the people tell the story of two brothers who created the world. They are To-Kabinana and To-Karvuvu, the twin gods of the Tolai people, and their story explains why the world contains both good and evil.

In the beginning, the two brothers lived alone in an empty world. To-Kabinana, the elder, was wise and thoughtful. He looked at the void and saw potential. To-Karvuvu, the younger, was foolish and careless. He looked at the void and saw nothing worth his attention.

To-Kabinana went to work. He carved a figure from wood and breathed life into it, creating the first man. He carved another figure and created the first woman. He taught them how to build houses, make fire, and plant gardens. He created the fish in the sea, the birds in the air, and the animals of the forest. Everything he touched became beautiful and useful.

To-Karvuvu watched his brother and tried to copy him. But his copies always went wrong. When he tried to create a man, he created a shark. When he tried to create a woman, he created a cassowary — a large, dangerous bird that kicks people to death. When he tried to create a coconut tree, he created a thorn bush. To-Karvuvu's creations were the first monsters, the first dangers, the first sources of suffering in the world.

To-Kabinana saw what his brother was doing and tried to stop him. But To-Karvuvu was stubborn. He introduced death into the world, teaching humans how to kill each other. He introduced cannibalism, teaching that the flesh of enemies could be eaten. He introduced lies, theft, and betrayal.

The argument between the two brothers became the fundamental conflict of existence. To-Kabinana represents the creative force, the impulse toward order, beauty, and life. To-Karvuvu represents the destructive force, the impulse toward chaos, ugliness, and death. Neither can destroy the other. They are locked in an eternal balance.

This is why the world contains both good and evil, according to the Tolai people. The wise brother and the foolish brother are both still working, both still creating, both still shaping the world. When you do good, you are helping To-Kabinana. When you do evil, you are helping To-Karvuvu. The choice is yours, but the outcome affects everyone.

The story of the twin brothers is found across the Pacific islands, from Papua New Guinea to Hawaii. Every culture has its version of the creative and destructive forces that shape existence. The Tolai version is remarkable for its stark honesty: good and evil do not come from outside the world — they are built into the very fabric of creation.
moral: Good and evil are woven into the fabric of existence; every choice tips the balance one way or the other.

source_references.title: The Story of a Melanesian Myth Cycle
source_references.author: G. Brown
source_references.notes: To Kabinana and To Karvuvu as creator twin deities in New Britain oral tradition

source_references.title: Bismarck Archipelago Ethnography
source_references.author: P. Schellong
source_references.notes: Early documentation of twin-god mythology in the Bismarck Archipelago

title: Thagyamin: The King of the Nats of Myanmar
summary: The highest spirit in Burmese folk religion rules over 37 nats from his heavenly throne.
full_text:
In Myanmar, where the golden pagodas rise above the tropical forests and the Irrawaddy River flows like a silver serpent through the heart of the land, the spirits are everywhere. They are called Nats, and there are thirty-seven of them, each with their own story. Above them all rules Thagyamin, the king of the Nats and the most powerful spirit in the Burmese pantheon.

Thagyamin is the Burmese version of the Hindu god Indra. He was adopted into Burmese Buddhism by King Anawrahta in the 11th century, when the king establishedTheravada Buddhism as the state religion. Anawrahta did not try to eliminate the traditional Nat worship — he incorporated it, placing Thagyamin at the head of the Nat pantheon as a protector of Buddhism.

Thagyamin lives in the Trayastrimsa heaven, a realm of perfect beauty and eternal spring. He rides a three-headed white elephant named Airavata, and he carries a conch shell and a yak-tail whisk as symbols of his authority. He is the king of all spirits, the ruler of the thirty-seven Nats, and the guardian of the Buddhist faith in Myanmar.

The other thirty-six Nats are all humans who died tragic deaths. They were princes murdered by rivals, queens driven to suicide, warriors killed in battle, maidens who died of broken hearts. Each Nat rules a specific domain and must be appeased with offerings. But Thagyamin rules them all, keeping order in the spirit world.

Thagyamin's most important duty is recording the deeds of humans. He keeps a book in which every good and bad action is recorded. At the end of each year, he reports to the higher gods, and the fate of each person is decided based on their actions. This is why Burmese people pray to Thagyamin before the new year, hoping to start fresh.

The festival of Thingyan, the Burmese New Year, is dedicated to Thagyamin. For three days, the country erupts in celebration — water fights in the streets, feasting, dancing. It is said that Thagyamin descends from heaven during Thingyan, recording the deeds of the people. The water thrown during the festival washes away sins from the previous year.

Thagyamin represents the blend of Buddhism and indigenous spirit worship that defines Burmese religion. He is proof that the old gods do not have to be destroyed when a new faith arrives — they can be transformed, elevated, and given new purpose. In the pagodas of Myanmar, Thagyamin stands guard, the king of the Nats and the protector of the faith.
moral: Old traditions and new faiths can coexist, each strengthening the other.

source_references.title: The Thirty-Seven Nats: A Phase of Spirit Worship Prevailing in Burma
source_references.author: R. C. Temple

title: Tin Hinan: The Queen of the Tuareg
summary: A legendary Berber queen fled Roman persecution and established the matrilineal traditions of the Tuareg people.
full_text:
In the heart of the Sahara Desert, in the Hoggar Mountains of southern Algeria, there is a tomb that holds the remains of the most important woman in Tuareg history. She is Tin Hinan, the queen who founded the Tuareg civilization. Her name means "She Who Comes From Afar," and her story is the origin myth of the blue-veiled people of the desert.

Tin Hinan lived in the 4th century AD, a time when the Roman Empire was expanding into North Africa. She was a Berber princess from the Tafilalet region of Morocco. When the Romans threatened her people, she gathered a group of followers and led them on a journey across the Sahara.

The journey was brutal. They crossed the Erg Chebbi, a sea of sand dunes that stretched for hundreds of miles. They survived on dates and camel milk. They followed the ancient wells that only the Berber guides knew. After months of travel, Tin Hinan reached the Ahaggar Mountains, a high plateau of black volcanic rock rising from the desert. She declared this place the new homeland of her people.

Tin Hinan established a society based on matrilineal principles. Among the Tuareg, descent is traced through the mother, not the father. Women own property, choose their husbands, and hold positions of religious authority. Men, not women, wear the veil. This was Tin Hinan's legacy.

In 1925, French archaeologists discovered a monumental tomb in Abalessa, near Tamanrasset. Inside, they found the skeleton of a woman lying on a bed of wood and leather. She was adorned with gold jewelry, pearl necklaces, and a crown of gold leaves. A second excavation in 1933 confirmed the finding. The tomb of Tin Hinan was real.

Today, Tin Hinan is the national symbol of the Tuareg people. Her image appears on everything from postage stamps to airline logos. The Tuareg refer to themselves as the "children of Tin Hinan." Her story is a reminder of the power of women in Berber society, the resilience of people who cross the harshest desert on earth, and the deep history of North Africa that predates both Islam and the European colonial presence.
moral: True leadership is not about power but about guiding people to a better place.

title: The Tomten's Christmas Vigil
summary: In the deep of a winter night, the Tomten — the ancient guardian spirit of the farm — makes his silent rounds while the household sleeps, watching over all creatures and reflecting on the mysteries of life.
full_text:
The snow lay deep over the land, drifted high against the walls of the farmhouse and smoothing the contours of the frozen fields into a single white expanse. The winter moon hung cold and bright over the forest, casting long blue shadows across the yard. Inside the farmhouse, the family slept — the farmer and his wife, their children snug under heavy quilts, the fire banked low in the hearth.

But someone was awake.

From the hayloft above the stable, a small figure emerged. He was no taller than a child of seven, with a long white beard that reached his chest and a face as wrinkled and wise as the bark of an ancient oak. He wore gray wool clothes, worn smooth by centuries of use, and a red pointed cap that drooped slightly to one side. The Tomten moved silently across the loft, his bare feet making no sound on the wooden boards.

The Tomten was old — older than the oldest person in the village remembered, older than the farm itself, older perhaps than the land. He had been here since the first tree was felled, since the first stone was laid. Some said he was the spirit of the first person who had cleared this land, bound to the farm forever. The Tomten himself had long forgotten. He only knew his duty.

He descended the ladder into the stable, where the horses stood in their stalls, breath steaming in the cold air. The Tomten moved among them, checking each one, his weathered hands stroking their flanks. He whispered to them in a language older than Swedish, and the horses nickered softly in reply. The cow lowed a greeting. The sheep stirred in their pen. The Tomten was their caretaker, their protector, the unseen guardian who ensured the animals thrived and the farm prospered.

In the chicken coop, the hens slept in a tight cluster. The Tomten counted them, noted the new cracks in the wall that would need mending, and made a mental note to remind the farmer — though how he would do this was his own secret.

He made his way back through the snow to the farmhouse. He did not feel the cold. He had not felt warmth or cold in centuries. He slipped through the door, invisible as the draft, and stood in the darkened kitchen. The remnants of the family's Christmas porridge sat in a bowl on the table — a gift left for him, as tradition demanded. The Tomten smiled, a rare expression that creased his weathered face. He dipped his finger in the porridge and tasted the butter melting at its center. Good. They remembered.

He climbed the stairs and looked in on the sleeping children. Their faces were peaceful in the moonlight, their breath soft and regular. The Tomten stood for a long moment, watching them. They would grow, he knew. They would leave this farm, have children of their own, and some of them would return. He had watched this pattern repeat for so many generations that he had lost count.

As the first gray light of dawn touched the eastern horizon, the Tomten returned to his hayloft. The rooster would crow soon. The farmer would wake. The daily rhythm of the farm would begin again. The Tomten settled into his corner, pulled his cap over his eyes, and listened to the sounds of the world waking around him. Another night had passed. The farm was safe. His duty was done.

This story is adapted from Viktor Rydberg's beloved 1881 poem 'Tomten,' one of Sweden's most famous literary works. Rydberg's poem transformed the traditional farm tomte from a sometimes capricious household spirit into a contemplative, almost melancholic figure — alone in the universe, keeper of ancient traditions. The poem was illustrated by Jenny Nyström, whose images of the white-bearded tomte in his red cap became the definitive visual representation of the Swedish jultomte (Christmas gnome) and influenced the modern image of Santa Claus worldwide.
moral: The truest guardians are unseen; they serve not for reward but because the duty itself is the reward.
source: Viktor Rydberg, Tomten (1881). Adapted from Swedish folklore traditions of the tomte.

title: Tales of the Trolls
summary: Three short tales of troll encounters: a peasant meets a troll at a spring, a girl is lured into a mountain by a handsome troll, and a farmer outwits a troll who steals his harvest. These tales reflect the Scandinavian belief in _bergtagning_ — being taken into the mountain by trolls.
full_text:
TALES OF THE TROLLS
       XIX
moral: Trolls may be powerful, but human wit and faith can overcome them every time.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XVIII: Tales of the Trolls. Communicated from mss. belonging to Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. Three folk-tales reflecting primitive faith and superstition; the first is also current in Norway. The later tales tell of women _bergtagen_ (taken into the mountain).

title: The Three Dogs
summary: A king falls under the spell of an evil witch-queen, and only his faithful queen and three magical dogs — with powers of strength, speed, and music — can break the enchantment. From Västergötland, this tale emphasizes the magic of music against trolls.
full_text:
THE THREE DOGS
       XXI
moral: The magic of loyalty and music can break even the strongest evil spell.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XX: The Three Dogs. Source: Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, p.195 (from Västergötland). Fairy tales hold a high opinion of music's power — the magic flute breaks the evil troll's spell, as in 'Faithful and Unfaithful'.

title: Torre Jeppe
summary: A mummified corpse named Torre Jeppe sits in a church night after night until a brave sexton discovers that the ghost cannot rest because of an ancient wrong that must be righted. A ghost-story founded on the belief that wrongdoing torments the guilty even after death.
full_text:
TORRE JEPPE
    XXVIII
moral: Even the dead cannot rest until old wrongs are made right.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXVII: Torre Jeppe. Retold and communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund after the mss. version of Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens. A ghost-story founded on the belief that a wrong torments the doer even after death.

title: The Troll Ride
summary: Peder Lars, a farmer's son, is riding to town to buy a new jacket before courting the beautiful and wealthy Lisa that evening. He is confident she will accept his proposal. But on the way through the forest, he encounters a hideous troll-woman lying injured in a ditch. She begs him to collect resin from seven pine trees to heal her wounded leg, offering gold coins in return. Peder Lars is repulsed by her appearance and initially refuses, but the haunting cries of the troll-woman's mother calling for her drive him to collect the resin. He tosses it at her in irritation and rides on. The delay causes him to be late: while en route, his brother meets him with terrible news — the rich miller Jonas has also come to propose to Lisa. With only minutes to spare and miles to go, Peder Lars' horse suddenly gains supernatural speed. Unseen, the grateful troll-woman rides behind him, her magic urging the horse forward at breakneck speed through the dark forest. Though his jacket is torn and his hair is wild, Peder Lars arrives at Lisa's house at the very last moment. Lisa, seeing his determined and disheveled appearance, finds him handsome and rushes into his arms. Peder Lars wins her hand, and from that day forward, he is never late again — because the troll-woman's magic ensures he always arrives on time.
moral: Even a reluctant act of kindness can be rewarded in unexpected ways.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollritten' (1910) by Anna Wahlenberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

source_references.title: Swedish Legends and Folktales
source_references.author: Gunnar Carlquist
source_references.notes: Swedish troll encounter narratives, including abduction rides on horseback

title: The Trolls and the Youngest Tomte
summary: In the world of Swedish tomtar (house gnomes), the youngest and most curious tomte listens with wonder as the older tomtar tell stories of their adventurous visits inside the trolls' mountain. The older tomtar warn of the dangers, but the youngest tomte's curiosity gets the better of him. He decides to venture into the trolls' mountain himself, despite the warnings. Inside the mountain, he discovers an open treasure chest guarded by two terrible trolls. The young tomte must use all his wits and courage to navigate the perilous troll realm and escape with his life. He narrowly avoids being captured and uses clever tricks to outsmart the trolls, who are large and powerful but not very intelligent. The story, beautifully illustrated by John Bauer (whose artwork for this tale is among his most celebrated), captures the tension between youthful curiosity and the wisdom of experience. The youngest tomte's adventure teaches respect for the dangers of the world while also celebrating the bravery required to explore the unknown. He eventually escapes with barely his life intact, learning a valuable lesson about the importance of heeding the warnings of elders.
moral: Curiosity can lead to dangerous adventures, and it is wise to listen to the counsel of those with more experience.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollen och tomtepojken' (1909) by Alfred Smedberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

source_references.title: Svenska Folksagor
source_references.author: Herman Hofberg
source_references.notes: Tomte-troll interaction stories from central Swedish oral tradition

title: The Troll Son Who Had Sun Eyes
summary: Humpe, the red-haired son of a troll mother, has large clear eyes with a gleam of sunlight in them, marking him as different from troll-kind. His unnatural affinity for daylight makes him an outcast. Filled with daylight's vigor, he leaves troll society and ventures into the forest. He encounters a birch maiden who, when he kisses her tree, transforms into a beautiful woman made of light. She accepts him despite his trollish appearance, and his love for sunlight transforms him, making him lose his troll-grey skin and become a handsome human forester.
full_text: Humpe is the red-haired son of a troll mother named Humpa, who is gentler than the other trolls. Unlike his kin, whose eyes are red and swollen, Humpe's eyes are large and clear with a gleam of sunlight in them, marking him as fundamentally different from troll-kind. His unnatural affinity for daylight makes him an outcast in the dark troll world — other trolls literally explode if exposed to too much sunlight, and they attempt to kill Humpe by keeping him awake all night so he will fatally encounter dawn. But Humpe proves immune; not only does sunlight fail to harm him, he can capture and reflect it through his eyes. Filled with daylight's vigor, he leaves troll society and ventures deeper into the forest. Initially self-conscious about his ugliness, he encounters personified nature in three stages: a forest spring where he sees his reflection and is encouraged to drink; a hazelnut tree that gently reprimands his presumption; and a birch maiden who, when he kisses her tree, transforms into a beautiful woman made of light. She accepts him despite his trollish appearance, telling him 'you have beautiful eyes, Humpe Trollson.' They fall in love, and she names him a forester. His love for sunlight transforms him: he loses his troll-grey skin, his hair becomes healthy brown, and he emerges as an admirably handsome human.
moral: True beauty lies not in appearance but in the light within one's eyes and heart.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1912 (Vilhälm Nordin). English summary in University of Texas thesis analysis (2014).

source_references.title: The Troll in Scandinavian Folk Narrative
source_references.author: Christiansen, R.T.

title: The Tomtarna
summary: The boy Andreas has left his childhood farm to seek fortune in the city. His aging parents miss him terribly. The farm tomte takes pity on them and sets out to find the boy. Andreas works as a guard in the troll prison until a troll bites off his fingers. In despair, he runs into the forest. The tomte finds him, produces the mother's apron, and tucks it into the boy's hand. Andreas dreams his mother is leading him home. When he wakes, the tomte has enchanted his hand with 'troll fingers' that work better than ever.
full_text: The boy Andreas has left his childhood farm to seek fortune in the city, like his five older siblings before him. His aging parents miss him terribly. The farm tomte (house gnome) takes pity on them and sets out to find the boy and watch over him secretly. Andreas works as a guard in the troll prison — a dangerous but well-paid job, hoping to earn enough to return home and restore the farm. One day a troll bites off the fingers on Andreas's right hand. In despair, he runs into the forest, ashamed to face his parents as a cripple. The tomte finds him sleeping, produces the mother's apron that he smuggled from the farm, and tucks a corner into the boy's hand. In his dream Andreas believes his mother is leading him home and follows willingly. When he wakes, the tomte has enchanted his injured hand, saying: 'Just think that you want to, for what one wants, one can.' Andreas now has 'troll fingers' and can work better than ever. He returns home joyfully and rebuilds the farm with the tomte's help.
moral: Home is not a place but the love that guides you back.

source_references.title: The Tomte: A Swedish Christmas Tradition
source_references.author: Aurelius, K.A.

title: The Treasure Seekers
summary: In Swedish folk belief, those who seek buried treasures must maintain absolute silence or the search will be in vain. Four men form a party to unearth treasures in Josäterdal. As they dig, supernatural illusions appear — an officer, soldiers firing at them, a red calf, a gallows. One digger wears a red shirt that catches the spirits' attention. When a voice cries out 'Shall we begin with him wearing the red shirt?' the man flees in panic.
full_text: It is an established rule in Swedish folk belief that those who seek buried treasures must maintain absolute silence, or the search will be in vain and harm will befall them, body and soul. Four men, aware of this, formed a party to unearth treasures said to be buried in Josäterdal. One midsummer night, as they crossed Lake Sälen, they saw a strange man approaching, dragging a large fir tree behind his boat. Shortly after, another man appeared asking if they had seen any float-wood. Recognizing these rowers as fairies (vättar), the treasure seekers pretended not to hear and continued to Josäterdal without incident. As they began digging into the hillside, a grand officer approached and addressed them, but no one answered. Soon soldiers marched up and fired at them, but they remained undeterred. The illusions escalated — a red calf appeared, the soldiers pressed closer until the diggers stood enveloped in powder-smoke so thick they could not see each other. Finally, a tall gallows was raised on the hillside. One digger wore a red shirt that caught the spirits' attention. A voice cried out, 'Shall we begin with him wearing the red shirt?' At this, the man lost his nerve and fled, the others following in panic. The tale illustrates the folk belief that treasure is guarded by supernatural beings who test the courage and resolve of those who seek it.
moral: Buried treasure is guarded not by locks but by the courage of those who seek it.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1890), Dalarne section.

title: Twigmuntus, Cowbelliantus, Perchnosius
summary: A clever farmer's lad determines to win the king's daughter by outwitting the king and his seven learned professors. On his journey, a twig hits him (Twigmuntus), a cow bellows (Cowbelliantus), and a perch bites his nose (Perchnosius). When the professors finish their learned gibberish, he stumps them with these three nonsense words.
full_text: A very learned king issued an edict: any man who could silence the king and his seven professors would win the princess and half the kingdom; those who failed would lose their heads. Many tried and died. A farmer's son studied with the parson but soon tired of books, saying luck matters more than learning. On his way to the palace, a twig struck his mouth—he called it 'Twigmuntus.' A cow bellowed—he called it 'Cowbelliantus.' A perch bit his nose as he swam a river—he called it 'Perchnosius.' At the palace, the professors delivered long learned harangues. The lad nodded to all of it. When asked to reply, he said he could prattle like them as a baby, then asked: 'Twigmuntus, Cowbelliantus, Perchnosius? Can you give me an answer to that?' The professors searched books, stared at the ceiling, meditated—but could not answer. Nor could the king. The lad won the princess and half the kingdom and ruled in his own way.
moral: Native wit can triumph over bookish learning; sometimes the simplest answer confounds the most learned.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Treasure in Säby Creek
summary: A miserly gentleman sinks his gold-laden wagon in Saby Creek rather than let it fall to others. A farmer raises white calves on skimmed milk to drag the treasure out but one calf is weakened by watered milk. The wagon slips back as a voice taunts about the calves.
full_text:
On the estate of Säby, in the parish of Hassle, lived, in former days, a gentleman
so rich that he could have purchased half of the territory of Vestergötland, but so
miserly that he could not find it in his heart to spend money for necessary food.

When he became aged, and knew that his life was drawing to a close, he began to ponder
what he should do with his wealth to prevent its falling into the hands of people
not akin to him, and finally he arrived at what he thought a wise determination.

One Sunday, when the people of the house were all in church, he loaded his gold and
silver upon a golden wagon and drew it down to Säby Creek, where he sank it in the
deepest hole he could find. Reaching home again, he felt more than usually content,
and laid himself down upon his bed, where he was found upon the return of the people
from church.

When a treasure has been concealed seven years, the Red Spirit is said to take possession
of it, and it is then called “Dragon’s property.” Over the spot where the treasure
lies a blue flame is seen to flutter at night time, and it is said the dragons are
then polishing their treasure.

When the seven years had passed the dragon light was seen over Säby Creek, now for
the first time revealing where the miser had deposited his wealth. Many efforts were made to recover the costly wagon and its load, but neither horses
nor oxen were found with strength enough to lift it from the hole.

About this time it happened that a farmer, returning from the market of Skagersholm,
where he had been with a load of produce, found quarters for the night with an old
man at Tveden. The evening conversation turned upon the hidden treasure, and the many
unsuccessful attempts to recover it that had been made, when the old man instructed
his guest to procure a pair of bull calves, upon which there should not be a single
black hair, and to feed them for three years on skimmed milk, whereby they would acquire
the necessary strength to drag the wagon out of the creek.

After great trouble the farmer was fortunate enough to find the desired white calves,
and he at once set about rearing them as instructed. But one time the girl who had
care of the calves accidentally spilled some of the milk set apart for one of them,
and, in order to have the pail full, she replaced the milk with water and gave it
to the calf as if nothing had happened. Meantime the calves grew up on their excellent
food to large and powerful oxen.

When they were three years old the farmer drove them to the creek and hitched them
to the golden wagon. It was heavy, but the calves put their shoulders to it, and had
raised it half way from the hole, when one of them fell upon his knees, and the wagon
sank back to its old resting-place. The farmer yoked them to it again, but just as
the wagon was about to be landed safely, the same bull fell to its knees a second time, so it went time after
time, until, finally, the owner saw that one of the bulls was weaker than the other.

When the wagon sank back the last time a bubbling and murmuring came up from the depths
and a smothered voice was heard to mutter:

“Your skimmed milk calves can’t draw my wagon out.” Whereupon the farmer understood
that to trouble himself further would be useless, since when no attempts have been
made to secure the treasure.
moral: Treasure hidden in secrecy is cursed and can never be recovered by force.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1888), translated by W.H. Myers.

title: The Trolls in Resslared
summary: Trolls in Resslared sabotage church bells until the Bishop of Skara consecrates one. The trolls then live in harmony with villagers borrowing food and repaying double. When new niggardly residents refuse a troll woman's requests their own provisions vanish and they are forced to leave.
full_text:
In a mountain called Räfvakullen, Fox Hill, near the Church of Resslared, Trolls,
it is said, have lived since long before the building of the church.

When the church was completed and the bell hung in the tower, the priest, as was the
custom, proceeded to read prayers over it to protect it from the power of the Trolls.
But his prayers lacked the expected efficacy, for he had not yet finished when the
Trolls took the bell and sunk it in the “Troll Hole” near the church.

A new bell was cast and hung, and this time the provost, who was more learned, was
selected to consecrate it. The provost also failed to hit upon the right prayers,
for the following Sunday, when the bell was about to be used for the first time, it
flew through the apertures in the tower and was broken on the roof of the church.

Again a bell was cast, and this time, as priest and provost seemed to be powerless
against the Trolls, the Bishop of Skara was sent for. His prayers were effectual,
and the bell was not again disturbed.

The Trolls thereafter dwelt in harmony with their neighbors, and especially with the
parishioners of Resslared. From the latter the Trolls were wont to borrow food and
drink, which they always returned two-fold.

In time the first residents died off, and new people took their places. The newcomers
were well provided with this world’s goods, even to being wealthy, but they were niggardly
and uncharitable.

One day the “mother” of the Trolls went, as was her custom of old, to a cottage, and
asked the housewife if she could lend her a measure of meal.

“No, that is out of the question! I have none in the house!” said the woman.

“Very well! It is as you say, of course,” replied the Troll, “but maybe you can lend me a can or
two of ale. My husband is away, and he will be very thirsty when he returns.”

“No, I can’t do that. My ale cans are all empty,” answered the housewife.

“Very good! Maybe you can lend me a little milk for my little child that is sick in
the mountain.”

“Milk! Where should I get milk? My cows are all farrow,” said the woman.

“Very well,” said the Troll woman, and went her way.

The housewife laughed in her sleeve, and thought that she had escaped the Trolls cheaply;
but when she inspected her larder it was found that she had really told the truth
to the Troll woman. The meal boxes were swept clean, the ale barrels were empty, and
the new milch cows, to the last one, farrow. Ever after that the plenty that had heretofore
been was wanting, until finally the people were compelled to sell out and move away.
moral: Those who refuse kindness to others even to supernatural beings will find their own prosperity vanish.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1888), translated by W.H. Myers.

title: The Troll Shoes
summary: An old witch prepares to fly to Blakulla by greasing her enchanted shoes. A weary traveler accidentally puts on the witch's shoes and is dragged head-downward through the night. A passerby knocks off one shoe which instantly vanishes and the weakened witch falls into what becomes the Troll Woman's Pit.
full_text:
Near Kölefors, in the jurisdiction of Kinda, lived, a long time ago, an old woman,
who, as the saying goes, was accustomed, during Easter week, to go to Blåkulla.

Late one Passion Wednesday evening, as was usual with witches, she lashed her pack
in readiness for the night, to follow her comrades in their wanderings. In order that
the start should be accompanied by as few hindrances as possible, she had greased her shoes and stood them by the fireplace
to dry.

In the dusk of the evening there came to her hut another old woman, tired and wet
through from the rain, and asked permission to remain over night. To this the witch
would not consent, but agreed to allow the woman to remain until she had dried her
soggy shoes before the fire, while she, unwilling to be under the same roof with her
guest, remained outside.

After a time the fire died out, and it became so dark in the hut that when the stranger
undertook to find her shoes, in order to continue her journey, she got and put on
the witch’s shoes instead. Hardly had she passed out through the door when the shoes
jerked her legs up into the air and stood her head downward, without, however, lifting
her into the air and carrying her away as would have been if the witch’s broom had
been in her hand.

In this condition the old woman and the shoes struggled through the night. Now the
shoes stood her on her head and dragged her along the ground, now the woman succeeded
in grasping a bush or root, and was able to regain her feet again for a time.

In the end, near morning, a man walking past, noticed her and hastened to her relief.
Answering her earnest pleading the man poked off one of the shoes with a stick, whereupon,
instantly, shoe and stick flew into the air and vanished in the twinkling of an eye.
After the adventures of the night the old Troll woman was so weakened that she fell
into a hole, which is pointed out to this day, and is called “The Troll Woman’s Pit.”
moral: Those who meddle with witchcraft will find their tools turned against the innocent.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1888), translated by W.H. Myers.

title: Taming the Shrew
summary: A comedic and satirical story about a headstrong woman who believes she is superior to all suitors. A clever youth uses unconventional psychological tactics and persistence to win her respect and affection, eventually leading to a harmonious marriage.
full_text: In a prosperous village lived a woman of such sharp tongue and fierce temper that no man dared ask for her hand. She claimed that no man was her equal in wit or strength. A young man, tired of the village's complaints and intrigued by the challenge, decided to woo her. Instead of flattering her, he mirrored her behavior, meeting her insults with laughter and her demands with playful contradictions. Every time she tried to intimidate him, he responded with a joke or a challenge that forced her to think rather than shout. Gradually, the woman found herself intrigued by this man who did not fear her. She began to admire his resilience and his ability to match her intellect. The battles of words turned into conversations of depth, and the 'shrew' discovered that she preferred a partner who could challenge her over one who merely obeyed her. They married in a celebration that the whole village attended, amazed that the fiercest heart had finally been tamed.
moral: True partnership is found when two equals challenge and respect one another.

title: The Three Princesses of Whiteland
summary: A brave youth journeys to the distant kingdom of Whiteland to win the hand of one of three princesses. He must pass three impossible tests of courage and wisdom, aided by the guidance of a mysterious forest spirit.
full_text: In the frozen north lay Whiteland, where the three princesses were said to be as cold as the ice they lived upon. To win their favor, a suitor had to bring a flower that bloomed in winter, a stone that floated on water, and a song that could wake the dead. Many princes failed and were cast into the dungeons. A poor shepherd boy, guided by a talking fox, learned the secrets of the land. The fox told him to find the Sun-Flower in the deepest cavern, to carve the Floating Stone from the Heart-Mountain, and to learn the Song of Life from the wind. The boy succeeded in all three tasks, not through strength, but by listening to the nature of the world. The princesses, touched by his humility and persistence, chose him as their champion. He married the youngest princess, and together they brought a permanent spring to the land of Whiteland.
moral: Humility and a willingness to listen to the world are the keys to overcoming impossible odds.

source_references.title: Folktales of Norway
source_references.author: Asbjørnsen, P.C. & Moe, J. (trans. Pat Shaw)

title: The Three Aunts
summary: A story about a young man who inherits a strange set of rules from his three aunts. To claim his inheritance, he must spend a year following their eccentric whims, leading to a series of surreal events and personal growth.
full_text: A young man named Erik inherited a vast estate, but only on the condition that he live with his three aunts for one full year. The aunts were peculiar: one only spoke in riddles, one only walked backward, and the third only ate foods that were blue. Erik found the conditions maddening, but the promise of wealth kept him there. Over the year, the aunts' oddities began to teach him lessons. The riddle-speaker taught him to look for hidden meanings; the backward-walker taught him to consider the past before moving forward; and the blue-eater taught him to find joy in the unusual. By the time the year ended, Erik no longer cared about the money. He had developed a deep affection for his aunts and a philosophical outlook on life. He used the inheritance to build a sanctuary for eccentric people, ensuring that no one would ever be judged for being different.
moral: Embracing the strange and unconventional in others can lead to the most profound personal growth.

source_references.title: The Types of the Norwegian Folktale
source_references.author: Hodne, Ø.

title: The Two Step-Sisters
summary: A kind-hearted girl is mistreated by her cruel stepmother and step-sisters, who force her to do all the hard work. Through her perseverance and the help of magical elements, she manages to overcome their malice, proving her inner beauty and virtue, and eventually finds her rightful place of happiness and honor.
full_text:
THE TWO STEP-SISTERS

Once on a time there was a poor man who had a daughter, and she was the loveliest child in the whole world. But when the girl was still a little child, her mother died, and her father took a wife; snip and this wife had two daughters of her own, and they were as ugly as two toads, and as cross as two sticks. Now the step-mother and her daughters had a great hate for the poor girl, and they treated her so badly that she was forced to do all the hard work in the house, while they sat and did nothing. 

One day the step-mother said to the girl, 'Go out and pick a bushel of lentils, but mind you don't leave a single one behind.' The girl went out and began to pick, but the step-sisters stood by and laughed at her, and they threw the lentils all over the ground, so that she had to start again and again. But the girl did not complain; snip she just kept picking until the bushel was full. 

Then the step-mother said, 'Now go and clean the whole house, and not a speck of dust must be left.' The girl worked and worked, but the step-sisters kept making a mess, throwing ashes and mud on the floor. But the girl did not give up; snip she cleaned and cleaned until the house was sparkling. 

At last the step-mother decided that the girl must go and seek the magic flower that grows on the highest peak of the mountain; snip for she knew that the flower could grant any wish, but it was a dangerous journey, and the step-mother hoped the girl would never return. The girl set off, and on her way she helped an old woman who was trapped under a fallen tree. In gratitude, the old woman gave her a magic ring and told her that whenever she was in trouble, she should just turn the ring and call for help. 

Eventually, the girl reached the peak and plucked the magic flower. When she returned home, the step-mother and step-sisters were furious that she had succeeded. They tried to steal the flower from her, but the magic ring protected her. The flower revealed the girl's true virtue and the step-sisters' cruelty to the whole village. The king, hearing of the girl's kindness and bravery, invited her to the palace and made her his queen, while the step-mother and her daughters were left to their own misery, forever haunted by the kindness they had tried to destroy.
moral: Inner beauty and kindness always triumph over outer ugliness and cruelty.

source_references.title: East of the Sun and West of the Moon
source_references.author: Asbjørnsen, P.C. & Moe, J. (trans. Theodore Hansen)

title: Tom Totherhouse
summary: A clever youth enters into a wager with a woman who is having an affair with a neighbor while her husband is deaf. Through a series of ingenious tricks and psychological manipulations, he forces the woman to confess her infidelity, winning the wager and exposing the truth.
full_text:
TOM TOTHERHOUSE

Once on a time there was a Goody who had a deaf husband. A good, easy man he was, but that was just why she thought more of the lad next door, whom they called 'Tom Totherhouse'. Now the lad that served the deaf man saw very well that the two had something between them, and one day he said to the Goody: 'Dare you wager ten dollars, mother, that I don’t make you lay bare your own shame?' 

'Yes I dare', said she; snip and so they wagered ten dollars. So one day, while the lad and the deaf man stood thrashing in the barn, the lad saw that Tom Totherhouse came to see the Goody. He said nothing, but a good while before dinnertime he turned toward the barn-door, and bawled out 'Halloa!' 

'What! are we to go home already?' said the man, who hadn’t given any heed to what the lad did. 'Yes, we must, since mother calls', said the lad. So when they got into the passage, the lad began to hem and cough, that the Goody might get Tom Totherhouse out of the way. But when they came into the room, there stood a whole bowl of custards on the table. 'Nay, nay, mother', cried out the man; snip 'shall we have custards to-day?' 

'Yes, that you shall, dear', said the Goody; snip but she was as sour as verjuice, and as cross as two sticks. So when they had eaten and drank all the good cheer up, off they went again to their work, and the Goody said to Tom: 'Deil take that lad’s sharp nose, this was all his fault; snip but now you must be off as fast as you can, and I’ll come down to you in the mead with a snack between meals.' 

This the lad stood outside in the passage and listened to. 'Do you know, father', he said, 'I think we’d best go down into the hollow and put our fence to rights, which is blown down, before the neighbours’ swine get in and root up our meadow.' 'Aye, aye, let’s go and do it', said the man; snip for he did all he was told, good, easy man. 

So when the afternoon was half spent, down came the Goody sneaking along into the mead, with something under her apron. 'Nay, nay, mother', said the man, 'it can’t be you any longer; snip are we to have a snack between meals too?' 'Yes, yes, that you shall', she said; snip but she was sourer and wilder than ever. 

So they made merry, and crammed themselves with bannocks and butter, and had a drop of brandy into the bargain. 'I’ll go off to Tom Totherhouse with a snack—shan’t I, mother?' said the lad. 'He’s had nothing between meals, I’ll be bound.' 'Ah! do; snip there’s a good fellow', said the Goody, who all at once got as mild as milk. 

As he went along the lad broke a bannock to bits, and dropped the crumbs here and there as he walked. But when he got to Tom Totherhouse he said: 'Now, just you take care, for our old cock has found out that you come too often to see our Goody. He won’t stand it any longer, and has sworn to drive his axe into you as soon as ever he can set eyes on you.' 

As for Tom, he was so frightened he scarce knew which way to turn, and the lad went back again to his master. 'There’s something wrong', he said, 'with Tom’s plough, and he begs you to be so good as to take your axe, and go and see if you can’t set it right.'
