Yes, the man set off with his axe, but Tom Totherhouse had scarce caught sight of him before he took to his heels as fast as he could. The man turned and twisted the plough round and round, and looked at it on every side, and when he couldn’t see anything wrong with it he went off home again; snip but on the way he picked up the bits of broken bannock which the lad had let fall. His old dame stood in the meadow and looked at him as he did this for a while, and wondered and wondered what it could be her husband was gathering up. 

'Oh, I know', said the lad, 'master’s picking up stones, I’ll be bound; snip for he has marked how often this Tom Totherhouse runs over here; snip and the old fellow won’t stand it any longer; snip and now he has sworn to stone mother to death.' 

Off went the Goody as fast as her legs could carry her. 'Ah! ah! don’t stone me to death; snip don’t stone me to death! and I’ll give you my word never to let Tom Totherhouse come near me again.' 

'Now the ten dollars are mine', bawled out the lad; snip and so they were.
moral: Dishonesty and betrayal will eventually be uncovered by a keen mind.

source_references.title: Danish Fairy and Folk Tales
source_references.author: Andersen, J. (ed. & trans.)
source_references.publisher: S. C. Griggs and Company, Chicago

title: The Twelve Wild Ducks
summary: A queen exchanges her twelve sons for a daughter, who grows up to seek her brothers. She discovers they are transformed into wild ducks and spends years weaving magical clothes to break their curse.
full_text: Once on a time there was a Queen who, desiring a daughter, traded her twelve sons to a Troll in exchange for a daughter, Snow-white and Rosy-red. As the princess grew, she learned of her lost brothers and set off to find them. She discovered they lived in a wooden hut and were transformed into wild ducks every morning. To break the spell, she had to pick thistle-down and weave twelve coats, shirts, and neckerchiefs without talking, laughing, or weeping. Despite the interference of a king who fell in love with her and a jealous stepmother who tried to frame her for eating her own children, the princess completed the task. Her brothers were restored to human form, and she lived happily with the king.
moral: Unwavering love and determination can overcome the most difficult of trials.

source_references.title: Folktales of Norway
source_references.author: Asbjørnsen, P.C. & Moe, J. (trans. Pat Shaw)

title: True and Untrue
summary: Two brothers, True and Untrue, leave home. Untrue blinds his brother and leaves him, but True recovers his sight and uses secrets learned from animals to win the king's favor and a princess.
full_text: Two brothers, True and Untrue, set out to find their fortune. Untrue betrays True by stealing his food and eventually plucking out his eyes. While blind, True climbs a tree and overhears animals discussing cures for the King of England's ailments: dew for his blindness, a toad for his daughter's muteness, and a spring under a stone for his water. True uses these secrets to cure the King and the Princess, gaining wealth and a royal marriage. Untrue eventually returns, pretending to be a beggar, but is outwitted by True and left with nothing, as his nature remains fundamentally untrue.
moral: Honesty and goodness are eventually rewarded, while deceit leads to ruin.

source_references.title: The Types of the Norwegian Folktale
source_references.author: Hodne, Ø.

title: Tatterhood
summary: A girl dressed in rags and underestimated by society possesses profound wisdom and courage. Through a series of trials, she solves complex riddles and saves a kingdom, proving that intellect and heart are more valuable than fine clothing.
full_text: There was once a girl who was so poorly dressed that everyone called her Tatterhood. She was mocked for her rags, but she possessed a mind sharper than any sword. One day, a prince was placed under a curse that only the most intelligent person in the land could break. Many lords and scholars tried and failed. Tatterhood, though dismissed by the court, stepped forward and solved the riddles that had baffled the wise. Through her insight and bravery, she broke the curse and restored the kingdom's peace. The prince, captivated by her wisdom and spirit, married her, and she became a beloved queen, teaching all that appearance is no measure of a person's worth.
moral: True value resides in character and intellect, not in outward appearance or social status.

title: Three Years without Wages
summary: A youth serves a miserly master for three years without pay, receiving only a puppy, a kitten, and a lizard as his wages. He discovers the animals are transformed royalty and, with their help, attains a magical ring and a kingdom.
full_text: A lazy youth was hired as a ploughboy by a rich man on the condition that he serve three years without wages. In return, the master promised to buy him three things—the first items he encountered in town on three separate mornings. The youth received a puppy, a kitten, and a lizard. He soon discovered the lizard was actually a prince under a witch's spell. After saving the prince, the youth was gifted a magical ring that granted any wish. Using the ring, he outsmarted the miserly master and the king, eventually marrying a princess and ruling a prosperous land, proving that patience and kindness are rewarded.
moral: Persistence and an open heart toward the unlikely can lead to rewards far greater than material wages.

title: The Tallow Candle
summary: A poetic story told from the perspective of a tallow candle, reflecting on the fleeting nature of life, light, and the inevitable end.

moral: Life is full of mysteries that demand our respect.

However, they were too slow, and as the sun rose over the horizon, the sunlight hit them, instantly turning them into the towering basalt sea stacks that still stand today as a warning against the power of the sun.

title: The Tale of Kagsagsuk
summary: Kagsagsuk is a mistreated orphan boy who suffers extreme cruelty from his foster mother and the village men. Seeking strength, he journeys to the mountains to encounter the Amarok, a giant solitary wolf spirit. Through a series of grueling trials where the Amarok repeatedly throws him down, Kagsagsuk sheds his weaknesses and gains superhuman strength. He eventually returns to the village, defeats three bears, and takes revenge on those who tormented him, though he remains a lonely figure of legend.
full_text: Kagsagsuk was an orphan, small and weak, who lived with a foster mother who despised him. She fed him scraps, dressed him in rags, and sent him to sleep in the dog pen where the huskies curled against the wind. The village men mocked him, calling him useless, and the children threw stones at him when he passed. But Kagsagsuk dreamed of strength. One night, as the northern lights painted the sky in curtains of green and violet, he decided to leave the village forever. He walked into the mountains, his thin clothes offering no protection against the wind, and climbed until he reached a high valley where the snow was stained red at dawn. There he met the Amarok, the great wolf spirit, larger than any bear, with fur gray as storm clouds and eyes like burning coals. 'You seek strength, little one?' the Amarok growled, its voice shaking the ground. 'Then let us begin.' The Amarok charged. Kagsagsuk tried to stand his ground, but the wolf threw him down the slope, tumbling over rocks and ice. He rose, bruised and bleeding. The Amarok threw him again, harder this time, and Kagsagsuk felt bones crack. 'Again!' the wolf commanded. Kagsagsuk rose once more, and on the third throw, he felt something shift inside him. Weakness fell away like old bones shedding flesh. His arms grew thick, his legs became pillars, and his heart hardened into a drum of war. 'Now you are ready,' said the Amarok, and vanished into the storm. Kagsagsuk returned to the village a changed man. He found three great bears terrorizing the settlement, and with his bare hands, he killed them all, stacking their pelts at the feet of the elders. His foster mother wept in terror. But Kagsagsuk did not harm her. He simply turned and walked away, leaving the village to its shame. He became a legendary figure of the wild, a protector of the helpless who never again trusted the kindness of men.
moral: True strength is forged through suffering; forgiveness is the ultimate power over those who wronged you.

source_references.title: Myths and Legends from Baffin Land

title: Tiggak the Shaman
summary: Tiggak is a powerful angakkoq (shaman) who becomes lazy and neglects his duties, causing friction with his brothers-in-law. During a harsh winter, the group becomes stranded on dangerous ice floes. Tiggak's supernatural foresight and magical abilities become the group's only hope for survival. By invoking charms and navigating the treacherous landscape with mystical guidance, he saves his companions, proving that his wisdom is indispensable despite his personal failings.
full_text: Tiggak was an angakkoq of formidable power, a shaman whose spirit could travel to the moon and whose word could calm the sea. But he was also lazy, preferring to sleep while others worked, and his brothers-in-law resented him deeply. 'Your magic does not fill our bellies,' they complained. The tension came to a head during a winter so brutal that the seals vanished. The brothers-in-law prepared to travel to a distant hunting ground, and Tiggak insisted on joining them. The journey was treacherous from the start. A storm swept in from the east, and the ice pack began to break apart. The men scrambled onto a drifting floe, their supplies lost, their kayaks smashed. As night fell and the wolves howled from the shore, the brothers-in-law began to despair. But Tiggak sat cross-legged on the ice and began to chant. The sky above them began to clear, and a beam of moonlight illuminated a path through the broken ice. 'Follow the light,' Tiggak said. They walked for hours, jumping from floe to floe. Whenever one of the brothers-in-law slipped, Tiggak caught them with a gesture. When a polar bear emerged from the darkness, Tiggak held up his hand and the bear turned and walked away. They reached the shore at dawn, exhausted but alive. The brothers-in-law never again spoke ill of Tiggak.
moral: Even imperfect people possess irreplaceable gifts; wisdom often comes in unexpected forms.

title: The Theft of Daylight
summary: In a time of eternal darkness, the world was cold and blind. Raven, the cunning trickster, discovered that a powerful being held the light of the world captured in a box. Through a series of disguises and tricks, Raven managed to steal the light and release it into the sky, creating the sun, moon, and stars, and bringing warmth and visibility to the Inuit people.
full_text: In the time before time, the world was wrapped in endless darkness. Raven saw the suffering and decided to help. He flew until he reached the house of a great chief who possessed three boxes containing the sun, the moon, and the stars. Raven transformed into a pine needle and floated into the chief's drinking water. The chief's daughter drank and became pregnant, giving birth to a baby boy who was Raven in disguise. The chief adored his grandson. One day, the baby pointed at the chest and cried until the chief opened it, releasing the stars. The next day, he released the moon. The next day, the sun blazed into the sky. The people wept with joy. Raven transformed back and flew away, cackling. And the light was in the sky, never to be put back in its box.
moral: Cleverness and patience can achieve what strength alone cannot.

source_references.title: Algonquin Legends of New England, or Myths and Folk Lore of the Micmacs, Passamaquoddies, and Tribes of Eastern Canada
source_references.author: Leland, Charles Godfrey

title: The Transformation of Raven
summary: An origin story explaining why the Raven is black. Originally, Raven had beautiful shimmering feathers, but through a series of mishaps and his own trickery, he was scorched by fire or covered in soot. This transformation serves as a permanent mark of his trickster nature.
full_text: Raven was not always black. He had feathers of every color — blues, greens, golds, reds, purples. He was the most beautiful bird in creation, and he knew it. His vanity was legendary. One day, Raven flew through a canyon and saw smoke rising from a crack. Curious, he flew closer, and soot blackened his wingtips. But his vanity drove him back to the fire again and again, trying to remove the soot, and each time more feathers turned black. Soon all his magnificent colors were gone. Raven wept, but the colors never returned. 'You were given beauty,' the spirits told him, 'but you used it only to serve yourself. Now black you shall remain, to remind all who look upon you that beauty without kindness is a hollow thing.'
moral: Beauty without kindness is hollow; vanity leaves a permanent mark.

title: Tikta'Liktak's Journey Home
summary: A young hunter becomes stranded on a drifting ice floe. The story chronicles his struggle for survival and encounters with Arctic spirits. His return home is a testament to navigation skills and the spiritual connection between a hunter and his homeland.
full_text: Tikta'Liktak set out alone, chasing a seal that led him farther from shore. When he looked up, the shore had vanished. The ice floe had broken free. For seven days he drifted, eating raw seal meat. On the third day, a walrus spoke: 'Follow the star that never moves.' On the fifth, a polar bear said: 'Do not eat all your meat at once.' On the seventh, a raven said: 'The wind will carry you home.' He navigated by the North Star, conserved his food, and read the wind. When the current brought him close to land, he jumped and swam. He collapsed at his mother's feet. He taught every young hunter to read the stars, conserve strength, and respect the ice.
moral: The land tests those who take it for granted; survival is earned through respect and knowledge.

title: The Tickling Demon Mahaha
summary: Mahaha is a thin, ice-blue demon who tickles victims with long nails until they die from lack of breath. However, the story concludes that Mahaha can be outwitted by the clever, who eventually trap him in a water hole.
full_text: Mahaha is a demon with skin the blue of old ice and claws as long as knives. He does not stab or crush. He tickles. He sneaks up on victims and tickles them until they cannot breathe, dying with a smile frozen on their face. But Mahaha has a weakness. He cannot resist hearing his name spoken backward. One clever hunter shouted 'AHAHAM!' into the darkness. Mahaha froze. The hunter ran to a water hole and jumped in. Mahaha followed, and the hunter swam through an underwater passage, emerging safely while Mahaha remained trapped beneath the ice.
moral: Even the most terrifying foe has a weakness waiting to be discovered.

title: The Tale of Njunje Paggas
summary: A Stállu tale collected by P. A. Lindholm and illustrated by John Bauer. Njunje Paggas is a Sámi hero who faces the Stállu in a battle of wits. The Stállu is a large, human-like creature who eats people. The Sámi hero wins through cleverness rather than strength. The Stállu's own knife must not be used against him, or he gains strength.
full_text: Njunje Paggas was a Sámi of ordinary size but extraordinary cleverness. When the Stállu began stealing reindeer from his family's herd, Njunje Paggas decided to confront the giant. He found the Stállu sitting outside his cave, gnawing on a reindeer leg. 'You have been taking my family's reindeer,' Njunje Paggas said. The Stállu laughed. 'I take what I want. What will you do about it, little man?' Njunje Paggas challenged the Stállu to a contest of wits. 'If I can trick you three times,' he said, 'you must leave our lands forever. If you trick me three times, I will bring you a hundred reindeer.' The Stállu agreed, confident in his cunning. The first trick: Njunje Paggas told the Stállu there was a golden reindeer in the forest and led him to a pond where the moon reflected like gold. The Stállu reached for it and fell in. The second trick: Njunje Paggas convinced the Stállu to let him tie him to a tree 'to keep him safe from the golden reindeer' while he 'searched for more.' The third trick: Njunje Paggas used his own knife, not the Stállu's, to carve a branch so it looked like the Stállu's head, convincing him he had been beheaded. The Stállu, terrified, fled the lands forever, and Njunje Paggas became a hero celebrated in stories and songs.
moral: Three clever tricks are worth more than an army of strong warriors.
source: P. A. Lindholm; Conrad, JoAnn (2020), Folklore 131:2

title: Trøllanes and the Troll House Party
summary: The settlement of Trøllanes on Kalsoy Island takes its name from ancient trolls who would come ashore once a year to wreak havoc—eating all available food, drinking, dancing, and behaving like wild party-goers. Terrified villagers would flee to Mikladalur every year, leaving their homes to be ransacked. One year, a woman was unable to escape and hid under a table as the trolls danced and feasted above her. In terror, she exclaimed 'Christ!' The trolls, hearing the holy name, fled from the village in panic and never returned. The route to the Kallur lighthouse begins in Trøllanes, offering some of the most dramatic views in the Faroe Islands.

title: Trøllkonufingur (The Troll Woman's Finger)
summary: A giant troll-woman from Iceland came to Vágar Island with the same plan as Risin and Kellingin: to steal the Faroe Islands and drag them to Iceland. Like the giants, she could only work at night. Caught by sunrise, sunlight turned her to stone. She toppled from the cliff into the sea—and was so enormous that her head became the island of Koltur (inhabited by only two people, Bjørn and Lukka Patursson), while one finger remained above water as the rock formation Trøllkonufingur near Sandavagur. Visible from the airport, it is a popular climbing spot. In 1844, a member of the Royal Danish entourage climbed it, descended, realized he'd dropped a glove, climbed back up—and was never seen again.

title: Tórur Rami and the Three Gifts of Mykines
summary: A giant named Tórur Rami tried to conquer the island of Mykines. The native inhabitant Óli Rami opposed him. When the giant approached, Óli Rami used magic to split Mykineshólmur from the main island, creating the islet now reached by a narrow metal bridge. The giant jumped across and they fought. Óli Rami won and spared the giant's life in exchange for three gifts: (1) a great whale would appear annually in Hvalagjógv bay—but the people complained and it stopped; (2) a giant magical tree—but the people complained it was shabby and it withered; (3) gannets (seabirds) that still nest only on Mykineshólmur. The condition was that the people must never complain about the gifts.

title: Tróndur í Gøtu (Tróndur the Viking Chieftain)
summary: Tróndur í Gøtu was a powerful 10th-century Viking chieftain who lived in Gøta on Eysturoy. He is the central figure in the Færeyinga Saga, which recounts his fierce opposition to the Christianization of the islands. He led pagan resistance against Sigmundur Brestisson, who was sent by King Olaf Tryggvason of Norway to convert the Faroese. After a series of battles, Sigmundur ultimately won and killed Tróndur. However, in folklore, Tróndur is often portrayed as a tragic hero—a symbol of Faroese independence and resistance against foreign domination. His burial mound is said to exist near Gøta.

title: The Troll Who Captured a Princess
summary: A classic Faroese folk tale collected by Jakob Jakobsen tells of a troll who lived in a cave and captured a princess to pick lice from his hair. Three brothers set out to rescue her. The elder two brothers failed, but the youngest—helped by the princess who threw him a ball of yarn to track the cave entrance—confronted the troll. The troll tried to trick the boy but was outwitted. When the troll gained entry to the king's house by picking the Faroese wooden lock (hvölpalás), the boy killed him. The treacherous brothers claimed credit, but the princess revealed the truth. The youngest brother married the princess and became king. Faroese trolls are creatures of darkness, large and ugly but easily outwitted.
